name: Sarvajnatman
dates_notes: Disciple-tradition links to Sureśvara via Devasvāmin/Deveśvara; the standard placement is 10th c. with some scholarship extending into the early 11th. Tightened from the older 900–1100 window.
core_thesis: Sarvajñātman, claiming descent from Sureśvara through Devasvāmin, builds the proto-*Vivaraṇa* Advaita that Prakāśātman will later systematise. The locus of *avidyā* is Brahman — pure *cit* — and not the *jīva* (*Saṃkṣepa-Śārīraka* 1.319–321). The *jīva* is itself *cidābhāsa*, a reflection (*pratibimba*) of Brahman cast in beginningless ignorance (*Saṃkṣepa-Śārīraka* 2.211–217; 3.1–10). Sarvajñātman gives this *bimba-pratibimba-vāda* its first systematic Advaitic statement, with the standard analogy: one face appears reflected in many mirrors without being multiplied, and so the Brahman-*bimba* appears as plural *jīva-pratibimbas* without compromising non-duality (*Saṃkṣepa-Śārīraka* 3.18–24). From this root three further commitments follow. The Mahāvākya is the unique *pramāṇa* productive of *akhaṇḍākāra-vṛtti* (*Saṃkṣepa-Śārīraka* 1.166–172, on *tat tvam asi*). *Karma* and *upāsanā* remove *vikṣepa* and *mala* but never produce *jñāna* directly; any strong *samuccaya* doctrine is therefore rejected (*Saṃkṣepa-Śārīraka* 3.336–345). Liberation is the *nivṛtti* of avidyā by knowledge alone (*Saṃkṣepa-Śārīraka* 4.42–55). Prakāśātman takes these positions over and refines them in the *Pañcapādikā-Vivaraṇa* (cf. *Vivaraṇa* on *Pañcapādikā* V–VI); they form the structural backbone of the post-Padmapāda Advaita line.

engaged_works.title: Samkshepa-Shariraka
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored verse-systematization of Śaṅkara's Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya.
engaged_works.summary: Four-*adhyāya* metrical *vārttika* in over 2,200 *anuṣṭubh* verses, compressing and argumentatively reconstructing Śaṅkara's *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*. Sureśvara's *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* and *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Vārttika* establish the broad principles of the Sureśvara line; the *Saṃkṣepa-Śārīraka* welds them into a *Brahma-sūtra*-shaped system. Four positions are established. First, Brahman is the locus of *avidyā*, explicitly against what will become the *Bhāmatī* alternative (*Saṃkṣepa-Śārīraka* 1.319–321). Second, the *jīva* is *cidābhāsa*, analysed by *bimba-pratibimba* (*Saṃkṣepa-Śārīraka* 3.18–24, 3.275–278). Third, the Mahāvākya is the unique productive *pramāṇa* of *akhaṇḍa-jñāna* (*Saṃkṣepa-Śārīraka* 1.166–172). Fourth, *karma* has no role in producing knowledge but retains a purificatory function (*Saṃkṣepa-Śārīraka* 3.336–345). The work generates a substantial commentarial tradition — Madhusūdana's *Saṃkṣepa-Śārīraka-Sāra-Saṃgraha*, Rāmatīrtha's *Anvayārtha-Prakāśikā*, Nṛsiṃhāśrama's *Tattvabodhinī* — and supplies the textual base with which later Advaita reckons.

engaged_works.title: Pancha-Prakriya
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally ascribed; ascription is generally accepted in tradition with limited modern critical engagement.
engaged_works.summary: Short pedagogical *prakaraṇa* organising the central themes of Sarvajñātman's Advaita under five *prakriyās*, the heuristic procedures by which the student is led from ordinary self-misidentification to recognition of the *akhaṇḍa* identity. The five-fold rubric typically tracks: the *avasthā-traya-viveka* analysing waking, dream, and deep sleep to isolate the witness; the *pañca-kośa-viveka* of the *Taittirīya*; the analytic location of *avidyā* in Brahman qua *cit*; the *bimba-pratibimba* exposition of the *jīva*; and the function of *Mahāvākya-vicāra* in producing the *akhaṇḍākāra-vṛtti*. The work matters as a compressed handbook of Sarvajñātman-line *prakriyā* whose categories recur in later Vivaraṇa pedagogy. Modern critical engagement remains limited; the ascription is traditional rather than colophon-secured.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
āśrayatvaviṣayatvabhāginī nirvibhāgacitir eva kevalā |
pūrvasiddhatamaso hi paścimo nāśrayo bhavati nāpi gocaraḥ ||

pada_analysis.gloss: of the darkness already established before

samasa_vigrahas.note: The compound compresses the two technical statuses of avidyā: locus and object.

karaka_structure.note: The later-arising conditioned self is the subject of bhavati.
Genitive relation: the darkness/avidyā that is prior to the later entity.

key_passages.english_close: Only undivided consciousness alone possesses the status of locus and object. For what is later-arising does not become, nor can it become, the locus or the object of the darkness that is already established before it.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: Avidyā belongs to partless consciousness itself; the conditioned jīva or Īśvara, which arise only after ignorance is operative, cannot bear it as its locus.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: beginningless nescience that veils and projects
the locus in which something resides

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is Sarvajñātman's cleanest programmatic statement of the Brahman-locus thesis: partless consciousness, not the later conditioned self, bears avidyā as both locus and object. Later Vivaraṇa-line arguments repeatedly return to exactly this claim, so the verse fairly represents the line's own self-definition rather than a detachable flourish.
key_passages.source_edition: N. Veezhinathan, The Samkṣepa-Śārīraka of Sarvajñātman: Critically Edited with Introduction, English Translation, Notes and Indexes, University of Madras, 1972 (archive scan: /orcd/home/002/eeshan/philosophy/materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/sarvajnatman_sanksepa_sariraka_veezhinathan_1972_archive.pdf).

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
sukṛtaduṣkṛtakarmaṇi kartṛtāṃ
matigatātmacitipratibimbakam |
vrajati tadvad adaḥ paramātmano
jagati yāti tamaḥpratibimbakam ||

pada_analysis.gloss: the reflection of self-consciousness that is in the intellect

samasa_vigrahas.note: The compound links consciousness to the mind/intellect as its reflecting medium.

karaka_structure.note: The intellect-reflection is the subject of vrajati.
The sphere in which agenthood is exercised.
The darkness-reflection of the supreme self is the subject of yāti.
Genitive relation: the reflection belongs to the supreme self.

key_passages.english_close: The reflection of self-consciousness, the one located in the intellect [buddhi], attains agenthood. It does so with respect to meritorious and demeritorious action. Likewise, that reflection in darkness — belonging to the supreme self — goes forth as Īśvara. It thus enters into relation with the world.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: Jīva is the intellect-conditioned reflection that becomes the doer of karma; Īśvara is the reflection in avidyā that stands in relation to the world-order.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: a reflected image, not an independent second original

key_passages.why_this_passage: This verse shows Sarvajñātman's reflection theory doing explanatory work: the jīva becomes a karmic agent through intellect-reflection, while the supreme self's darkness-reflection explains worldly lordship. It therefore represents not a decorative metaphor but the operational heart of his bimba-pratibimba model.
key_passages.source_edition: N. Veezhinathan, The Samkṣepa-Śārīraka of Sarvajñātman: Critically Edited with Introduction, English Translation, Notes and Indexes, University of Madras, 1972 (archive scan: /orcd/home/002/eeshan/philosophy/materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/sarvajnatman_sanksepa_sariraka_veezhinathan_1972_archive.pdf).

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
upādhim aupādhikam āntaraṃ cidābhāsanaṃ citpratibimbakaṃ ca /
cidbimbam evaṃ caturaḥ padārthān vivicya jānīhi tadarthabhājaḥ //
tathā tvamarthe 'pi catuṣṭayaṃ tad vivecanīyaṃ nipuṇena bhūtvā /
matiś cidābhāsanam evam asyāṃ bimbaṃ tadīyaṃ pratibimbakaṃ ca //

pada_analysis.gloss: referents, entities meant by the word

karaka_structure.note: Objects of jānīhi in the first half.
The fourfold set in the meaning of tvam is what must be discriminated.
Semantic domain: the meaning of the word tvam.
The consciousness-manifestation occurs in this intellect.

key_passages.english_close: [On the 'tat' side]: there are the upādhi, the inner upādhi-conditioned manifestation of consciousness (cidābhāsa), the reflection of consciousness (cit-pratibimba), and the original consciousness-image (cit-bimba). Discriminate and know, thus, these four referents that bear the meaning of 'tat'. Similarly, in the case of the meaning of 'tvam' too, that fourfold set is to be discriminated — once one has become exact in analysis. There it is the intellect (mati), the manifestation of consciousness (cidābhāsa) in it, the original (bimba) in that, and the reflection (pratibimba) of that original.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: Before one states the identity taught by the mahāvākya, one must sort out the four layers involved on each side of the sentence.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: a limiting adjunct that conditions appearance without altering the underlying reality

key_passages.why_this_passage: Rather than extracting only the conclusion of identity, this passage lays out the semantic furniture Sarvajñātman thinks must be distinguished before interpreting the mahāvākya. It is therefore a fair representative passage for his method, because ontology and sentence-meaning are coordinated from the outset instead of being treated as separate topics.
key_passages.source_edition: N. Veezhinathan, The Samkṣepa-Śārīraka of Sarvajñātman: Critically Edited with Introduction, English Translation, Notes and Indexes, University of Madras, 1972 (archive scan: /orcd/home/002/eeshan/philosophy/materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/sarvajnatman_sanksepa_sariraka_veezhinathan_1972_archive.pdf).

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
upādhir antaḥkaraṇaṃ tvamarthe
jīvatvam ābhāsanam atra tadvat /
tadanvitā cit pratibimbam evam
ananvitāṃ bimbam āhuḥ ||

karaka_structure.note: The whole verse explains the referents included under the word tvam.
Implicit authorities/teachers are the speaking subject.
What is said to be the prototype.

verb_modality.note: Perfect with present-force reporting an established doctrinal statement.

key_passages.english_close: In the meaning of 'tvam', the limiting adjunct is the internal organ; the manifestation there is jīva-hood, just as in that other case. Consciousness connected with that is the reflected image; consciousness unconnected with it, they say, is the original image.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: The jīva is not the original consciousness itself but consciousness as reflected through the internal organ and marked by individual-selfhood.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: the internal organ of cognition, especially intellect
the condition of being an individual embodied self

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is the clearest compact statement in the available corpus of Sarvajñātman's account of the tvam-side: the jīva is consciousness reflected in the internal organ, while the original consciousness remains unassociated. It fairly represents his position because later discussion in the chapter expands this structure rather than replacing it with a different model.
key_passages.source_edition: N. Veezhinathan, The Samkṣepa-Śārīraka of Sarvajñātman: Critically Edited with Introduction, English Translation, Notes and Indexes, University of Madras, 1972 (archive scan: /orcd/home/002/eeshan/philosophy/materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/sarvajnatman_sanksepa_sariraka_veezhinathan_1972_archive.pdf).
